1.description: Choice set. This figure shows a snapshot fromhttps://www.moneysupermarket.com/mortgages/of the five cheapest mortgages offered in a market after filling in information about the value of the property and the loan amount. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Risk weights across lenders and LTVs. This figure shows the average risk weights for two groups of lenders at different LTVs. IRB includes all lenders in the sample used for the structural estimation that adopt an IRB model in calculating capital requirements. The internal model of the lender is subject to supervisory approval. The distribution of IRB risk weights within each LTV band is represented by a Tukey boxplot, where the box represents the interquartile range (IQR) and the whiskers represent the most extreme observations still within 1.5×IQR of the upper/lower quartiles. SA includes all lenders in the sample used for the structural estimation that adopt the standardized approach. For the latter group, the risk weights are set by the regulator in a homogeneous manner across banks and vary between 35% and 45% based on the LTV of the loan. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Pricing risk and regulation. This figure shows the conditional interest rate from the regressionrj=controlsj+∑j=6095ltvj+εj, wherecontrolsjare fixed effects for time, interest rate type, and lenders andltvjare 0.5 percentage points LTV bins. The dotted vertical lines denote the maximum LTV of 60%, 70%, 75%, 80%, 85%, 90%, and 95%. The blue and red lines plot the schedule for a representative large lender adopting the IRB model and a representative small lender using the standardized approach. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Model change. Panel (A) shows the relative risk-weight, while Panel (B) shows the relative interest rate of a maximum LTV of 95% relative to a maximum LTV of 70% for a treated lender before and after the adoption of an IRB model and a control lender that continues to adopt the standardized approach. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Originations across LTVs. Panel (A) shows the share of mortgages originated at different LTV bins for first-time buyers. Each bin is 0.5 percentage point wide. The blue line shows the plain distribution where the LTV is computed as the ratio between the loan value divided by the house value. The red line controls for fees by subtracting the fees added to the loan from the loan value. The dotted vertical lines denote maximum LTVs of 60%, 70%, 75%, 80%, 85%, 90%, and 95%. Panel (B) shows the portfolio shares for a representative large lender adopting the IRB model and for a representative small lender using the standardized approach. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Originations across lenders. Panel (A) shows the price and market share of two products for first-time buyers offered by two different lenders with the same initial period (two years), the same maximum LTV (60%) and similar quantities (£140,000 to 160,000). The price is the full APR, which includes the initial interest rate and origination fees. The market share is computed as the fraction of people who buy a given product in a specific quarter over the total number of mortgage borrowers in that quarter. Panel (B) shows the correlation between the share of branches and the share of mortgages across postcode areas in the United Kingdom for the six largest lenders. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Performance across lenders and LTVs. Panel (A) shows the mean, 5thpercentile, and 95thpercentile of the distribution of mortgages in arrears for different LTV brackets for lenders using an IRB model and for lenders using a standardized approach to compute risk weights. Panel (B) shows the mean, 5thpercentile, and 95thpercentile of the distribution of mortgages that are refinanced (i.e., not paying the SVR) for different LTV brackets for lenders using an IRB model and for lenders using a standardized approach to compute risk weights. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Counterfactual risk-weighted capital requirements. The figure shows the distribution of risk weights in the two counterfactual scenarios for capital requirements. In Panel (A), I show the first counterfactual, in which all lenders adopt the standard approach for setting risk weights. In Panel (B), I show the second counterfactual, in which I compute the mean risk-weight across lenders using the internal model and assign it to the small lenders using the standardized approach. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Counterfactual leverage limits. The figure shows the market shares of mortgages in the two counterfactual scenarios for leverage regulation. In both counterfactuals, I impose a maximum leverage limit of 90%, by excluding products with a maximum leverage above 90% from the choice set of all borrowers. In Panel (A), I show the counterfactual with homogeneous capital requirements, and in Panel (B), I show the equilibrium with risk-weighted capital requirements. [Colour figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
